FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=8292, 21 sites nationwide, baseline ages 9-10, Years 2-4 follow-up (2018-2020); NIH-funded; recruitment described as 'large, diverse sample of 9-10-year-olds in the U.S. at baseline (2016-2018) from 21 sites nationwide.'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal mediation analysis using ABCD Study data; generalized structural equation modeling (GSEM) estimating direct and indirect effects of social media time (Year 2) on nicotine experimentation (Year 4) via smoking expectancies (Year 3).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in The American Journal on Addictions (2026); no indication of being a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: 'social media time' (hours/day, weekday/weekend weighted average) is a phone/SM-specific variable appearing as IV with nicotine experimentation (substance use) as the individual-trait-level DV. Direct effect B=0.14 (95% CI: 0.08-0.20, p<.001) in Table 2; B=0.15 (95% CI: 0.10-0.21, p<.001) in negative expectancies model. Nicotine experimentation is individual-trait-level (substance use behavior).

FT_IC3.evidence: GSEM direct and indirect path coefficients reported: direct effect B=0.14 (SE=0.029, p<.001); indirect effect through positive smoking expectancies B=0.009 (SE=0.003, p=.002); proportion mediated 5.97% (95% CI: 1.27%-10.67%, p=.013) — Table 2.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported including regression coefficients, standard errors, 95% CIs, p-values, and proportion mediated for the SM-nicotine experimentation relationship (Table 2, Figure 1).

FT_EC7_reason: Social media time is IV (predictor) in the primary analyses, with nicotine experimentation as DV. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis; here SM is IV. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Longitudinal prospective cohort study (Years 2-4). Social media time at Year 2 predicts nicotine experimentation at Year 4, with positive smoking expectancies (Year 3) as mediator. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 5 (2021-2023), N=11,530; 21 demographically diverse sites; baseline 2016-2018; ABCD Study 6.0 release. Methods confirm U.S. NIH-funded ABCD Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Year 5 data; multivariable linear regression estimating association between dating app use and depression symptoms (CBCL DSM-Oriented Affective Problems Scale).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in BMC Research Notes (2026); distinct from a prior Nagata et al. 2024 BMC Res Notes paper that examined prevalence of dating app use in early adolescents (different wave, different outcome); this paper examines depression symptoms at Year 5. No indication of exact duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: 'online dating app use' (ever used a dating app; yes/no) is an online dating app variable — a qualifying phone/SM variable under the criteria ('online dating apps / hookup apps'). IV = dating app use; DV = depression symptoms (CBCL DSM-Oriented Affective Problems Scale), an individual-trait-level mental health outcome. Adjusted ß=0.42 (95% CI: 0.08-0.76, p=0.017) reported in Table 2.

FT_IC3.evidence: Standardized beta coefficient ß=0.42 (95% CI: 0.08-0.76, p=0.017) for dating app use predicting depression symptoms (Table 2); unadjusted ß=0.47 (p=0.009); sensitivity analysis ß=0.44 (p=0.035); parallel analysis with internalizing symptoms ß=0.66 (p<0.001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative estimates reported including standardized betas, 95% CIs, standard errors, and p-values across primary and sensitivity analyses for the dating app use - depression symptoms relationship.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (Year 5 cross-sectional data); EC7 requires longitudinal design. Cross-sectional design alone disqualifies EC7. Additionally, dating app use is IV, not DV.
notes: Cross-sectional study of 13-16-year-olds (ABCD Year 5). Dating app use (qualifying online dating app variable) associated with depression symptoms. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study first wave data; 'data from the first wave of the ABCD study (Garavan et al., 2018), an ongoing project seeking to recruit 11,500 9-10 year olds'; final sample N=3847; NIH-funded, 21 sites, U.S. nationally representative sample.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data using multiple machine learning regression approaches (LASSO, ridge, elastic net, linear regression) to estimate relative importance of 43 variables for %BMIp95.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Psychological Medicine (2020); no indication of being a duplicate of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable found. The only screen-related variable in all results tables and figures is 'screen time,' defined in Methods as 'the average minutes per week a child typically spends on a computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device' — a multi-device aggregate bundling computer, cellphone, tablet, and other electronic devices with no per-device breakout anywhere in the paper (main, supplementary, sensitivity). Step 2: Composite fails — includes non-phone/non-SM ingredients (computer, tablet, other electronic devices) without separate breakout. Per binding clarification §2.4 rule 5: multi-device aggregated time fails IC2.

notes: EXCLUDE (FT-EC2). Screen time is defined as multi-device aggregate (computer + cellphone + tablet + other electronic device) with no per-device breakout. Fails IC2 Step 2 under binding multi-device aggregation rule.
